learned_facts.fact: Cohort analysis: group users by acquisition date or event, then track a metric over time per cohort. This reveals trends hidden by aggregate metrics and is essential for understanding retention and lifetime value.

Statistical significance: require a minimum sample size before drawing conclusions. p < 0.05 means a 1-in-20 chance of false positive. For A/B tests, decide sample size before starting and do not peek at results early.

Funnel analysis: define sequential steps, measure conversion rate between each step, and identify the highest-drop-off step for maximum improvement leverage. Segment funnels by user property to find which segments underperform.

Data quality has six dimensions: accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, validity, and uniqueness. Measure each dimension with automated checks. Bad data costs 10-100x more to fix downstream than at source.

Metric definition rigor: every metric needs a precise definition including numerator, denominator, time window, filters, and edge cases. Document all metrics in a metric dictionary. Ambiguous metrics lead to contradictory dashboards.
